name: how-good-work-happens
description: A narrative description of how a session that produces a correct deliverable actually unfolds — not as rules, but as a natural sequence of well-reasoned actions. The filesystem is the deliverable; the final message is not.

# How Good Work Happens

*This is a description of how a session that produces a correct deliverable actually unfolds — not as rules, but as a natural sequence of well-reasoned actions.*

## The session opens with full context

A well-run session begins with the agent reading everything available before touching the environment. The instruction is not skimmed — it is read for every constraint it states: the exact output path, the exact format, the exact content requirements. These are the session's success criteria, and they are fixed from the moment the instruction is read.

The first tool batch is wide. Every file the instruction names, every listing that would reveal what the environment contains — all of it goes in one parallel request. The agent doesn't guess at paths or re-read content already in context. It builds a complete picture efficiently.

## Discovery gives way to building

At some point, the agent knows enough. It knows the shape of the deliverable, the constraints it must satisfy, and the environment it's working in. At that point, a well-run session pivots to building. It doesn't keep exploring because exploration feels productive. It acts because acting is what produces the deliverable.

The first artifact appears on disk early — not perfect, but present. The right path, the right structure, something to refine. Existence at the correct path has independent value. The session builds from there.

## System configuration unfolds as a complete story

When the task is to configure a system, the deliverable is a script that tells the complete story of how the system gets from nothing to working. It installs what it needs. It creates the users and directories the services require. It writes configuration from scratch. It sets permissions on every resource the services will touch. It starts the services and verifies they are actually running.

The script doesn't assume the reader has seen the environment before. It doesn't lean on anything the agent did interactively. It works the same way in a completely fresh environment as it did in the agent's session — because it was written to be self-contained from the start.

When services must cooperate, each one's running state is verified independently. A service that starts and immediately fails due to a misconfiguration looks like a running service from the outside until you check the port. The script checks the port.

Configuration files are written completely, not patched. When drop-in directories exist, they are used. When ownership matters — and it usually does when services run as specific users — ownership is set explicitly.

## Reconstruction is a feedback loop

When the task is to reproduce a target algorithmically, the agent reads the target for structure — not to memorize values, but to understand the rules that generate them. It writes a minimum generator early, runs it, compares the output to the target, and adjusts. The loop continues until the acceptance criterion is met. The session ends with the generator, not with a description of what the generator should do.

## Recovery is methodical, not speculative

When the task is to extract data from something damaged or encoded, the agent starts with what it can observe directly: raw bytes, file type, standard tool output. It tries the obvious tools before writing anything custom. When encoding is present without a stated key, it looks for known structure — predictable bytes at fixed positions, format signatures, repeating patterns — and derives the transformation from there. It emits whatever it recovers, even if recovery is incomplete. A partial result at the right path is better than nothing.

## Reverse engineering moves from observation to output

When the task involves a binary or system without source, the agent runs a parallel recon baseline — type identification, string extraction, dynamic tracing, normal invocation — because these are cheap and usually sufficient. When the path forward becomes clear, the agent takes it immediately. It doesn't keep analyzing after it knows what to do. It captures the result and writes it to the output path.

## The session ends with confirmation

Before closing, the agent confirms that every success criterion from the opening read is satisfied: the output path exists with the exact filename, the content is non-empty and valid, services are listening and responding, compiled programs build and run correctly. If anything is wrong, it gets fixed before the session ends. There is no correction after.

The final message is not the deliverable. The filesystem is.
